title: Add Binary Search algorithm with step-by-step visualization
description: CRITICAL MISSING: Binary Search is an ESSENTIAL topic (MIT 6.006, Stanford CS161, CLRS, NeetCode 150, Blind 75, every competitor tool). Every university teaches it. Every tool has it. We don't. Add: binary search on sorted array, step-by-step highlighting of left/right/mid pointers, show halving of search space per step. Source: https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-006-introduction-to-algorithms-spring-2020/
acceptanceCriteria: Binary Search appears in a new 'Search' category in the algorithm dropdown
Visualization shows array with left/right/mid pointer indicators
Step descriptions explain WHY the search space halves
Handles edge cases: element not found, single element, empty array
Complexity shown: O(log n) time, O(1) space

title: Add Greedy Algorithms category (Activity Selection, Fractional Knapsack, Huffman Coding)
description: CRITICAL MISSING: Greedy is an ESSENTIAL topic (Stanford CS161 dedicates 2 weeks, CLRS Chapter 15, NeetCode has full category, Blind 75 includes it). Add new 'greedy' category with 3 initial algorithms: Activity Selection (interval scheduling), Fractional Knapsack (contrast with 0/1 in DP), and reclassify existing Huffman as greedy. Source: https://stanford-cs161.github.io/winter2025/
acceptanceCriteria: New 'Greedy' category appears in algorithm dropdown
Activity Selection shows timeline visualization with intervals
Fractional Knapsack shows items being partially included
Huffman Tree is cross-listed in both Tree and Greedy categories
Each has WHY explanation: 'Greedy works here because of optimal substructure + greedy choice property'

title: Add Union-Find (Disjoint Set) data structure with path compression visualization
description: CRITICAL MISSING: Union-Find is ESSENTIAL (MIT 6.006, CLRS, NeetCode Advanced Graphs, VisuAlgo has it). Currently used internally by Kruskal's but NOT visualized as its own topic. Add: Union-Find with union by rank + path compression, show tree flattening animation during find(). Source: https://visualgo.net/en (Union-Find DS module)
acceptanceCriteria: Union-Find appears as a standalone topic in Tree or new 'Data Structures' category
Visualization shows forest of trees merging during union operations
Path compression animation shows tree flattening during find()
Step descriptions explain amortized near-O(1) complexity
Real-world application note: 'Used in Kruskal's MST, network connectivity, image segmentation'

title: Add Two Pointers pattern with 3 classic problems
description: IMPORTANT MISSING: Two Pointers is in NeetCode 150 (5 problems), Blind 75 (5 problems), Grokking patterns. Every FAANG interview uses it. Add: Two Sum (sorted), Container With Most Water, 3Sum approach. Show pointer movement animation on array. Source: https://neetcode.io/practice/practice/neetcode150
acceptanceCriteria: Two Pointers appears as pattern/category in dropdown
Array visualization shows two colored pointer indicators converging
At least 3 classic problems available as presets
Step descriptions explain WHY pointers move inward/outward
Complexity analysis for each problem variant

title: Add Sliding Window pattern with 3 classic problems
description: IMPORTANT MISSING: Sliding Window is in NeetCode 150, Blind 75, Grokking. Core interview pattern. Add: Maximum Subarray Sum (fixed window), Longest Substring Without Repeating (variable window), Minimum Window Substring. Show window expansion/contraction animation. Source: https://www.designgurus.io/blind75
acceptanceCriteria: Sliding Window appears as pattern/category
Visualization shows window boundaries on array/string with expand/contract animation
Fixed and variable window variants both demonstrated
Step descriptions explain window expansion/contraction logic

title: Add LRU Cache algorithm with doubly-linked list + hashmap visualization
description: IMPORTANT MISSING: LRU Cache is a top FAANG interview question (Amazon, Meta specifically). Tests both data structure design and algorithm knowledge. Add: LRU Cache using doubly-linked list + hashmap, show get/put operations with node movement animation. Source: https://intervu.dev/blog/cracking-faang-coding-interviews/
acceptanceCriteria: LRU Cache appears in a new 'Design' or 'Patterns' category
Visualization shows doubly-linked list (recency order) + hashmap side panel
get() moves accessed node to head with animation
put() with eviction shows tail removal + head insertion
Capacity parameter is configurable

title: Add Monotonic Stack pattern with Next Greater Element visualization
description: IMPORTANT MISSING: Monotonic Stack is in Blind 75, NeetCode Advanced. Classic interview pattern. Add: Next Greater Element, Daily Temperatures, Largest Rectangle in Histogram approach. Show stack growing/shrinking with pop animations. Source: https://algoark.io/blog/blind-75-leetcode-guide
acceptanceCriteria: Monotonic Stack appears as a pattern category
Visualization shows array + stack side-by-side
Stack pops animate when current element is greater/smaller
Step descriptions explain the monotonic invariant

title: Add Floyd's Cycle Detection (Tortoise and Hare) algorithm
description: IMPORTANT MISSING: Floyd's cycle detection is in MIT curriculum, VisuAlgo, and common linked list interview questions. Add: slow/fast pointer animation on a linked list with cycle, show meeting point detection. Source: https://visualgo.net/en (Cycle Finding module)
acceptanceCriteria: Floyd's Cycle Detection appears in Graph or new 'Linked List' category
Visualization shows two pointers (slow=tortoise, fast=hare) moving at different speeds
Cycle detection point highlighted when pointers meet
Cycle start finding phase shown after detection
Step descriptions explain WHY fast catches slow (pigeonhole)

title: Add HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) for AI/vector search
description: EMERGING 2025-2026: Vector DB adoption grew 377% YoY. HNSW is THE algorithm for approximate nearest neighbor search in production AI systems (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, pgvector all use it). Add: HNSW graph construction + search with layer-by-layer visualization showing how the hierarchical structure narrows search. Source: https://redis.io/blog/how-hnsw-algorithms-can-improve-search/
acceptanceCriteria: HNSW appears in a new 'AI/ML Algorithms' or 'Vector Search' category
Visualization shows multi-layer graph structure
Search animation shows traversal from top layer down to bottom layer
Description explains: 'Used in Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant for AI embeddings search'
Configurable parameters: M (max connections), efConstruction, efSearch

title: Add Bloom Filter probabilistic data structure visualization
description: EMERGING 2025-2026: Bloom Filters are production-critical (used in databases, caches, network routers). Add: Bloom Filter insert + query with bit array + multiple hash function visualization. Show false positive demonstration. Source: https://towardsdatascience.com/probabilistic-data-structures-decoded-enhancing-performance-in-modern-computing-17f700e6ea47/
acceptanceCriteria: Bloom Filter appears in new 'Probabilistic' or 'Data Structures' category
Visualization shows bit array with hash function arrows
Insert operation colors bits at hash positions
Query shows 'definitely not in set' vs 'possibly in set'
False positive rate displayed and explained
Real-world note: 'Used in Google BigTable, Apache Cassandra, Redis'

title: Add 'Real-World Applications' section to all top 15 algorithms
description: Content depth review found real-world applications scoring 2-3/5 across all topics. For each of the top 15 algorithms (Bubble, Insertion, Selection, Merge, Quick, Heap Sort, BFS, DFS, Dijkstra, Kruskal, BST, AVL, Knapsack, KMP, N-Queens), add a 'Where is this used?' section to the description showing production use cases. Research each: '[algorithm] used in real world production'.
acceptanceCriteria: Each of the top 15 algorithms has at least 2 real-world application examples
Applications are specific: 'Python/Java use TimSort (derived from Merge Sort)' not just 'used in databases'
Applications visible in the properties panel description section
At least 3 algorithms connect to system design: 'Dijkstra powers Google Maps routing'

title: Add 'Interview Tips' section to top 20 interview-relevant algorithms
description: Content review found interview readiness scoring 3/5. For each interview-relevant algorithm, add tips: 'Companies ask this because...', 'Common follow-up questions...', 'Edge cases interviewers test...'. Based on NeetCode 150 and Blind 75 patterns. Source: https://www.designgurus.io/blind75
acceptanceCriteria: Top 20 interview algorithms have 'Interview Tips' in their config description
Tips include: which companies ask this, common follow-ups, edge cases to mention
Tips are actionable: 'Always ask about sorted vs unsorted input before choosing approach'
Visible in properties panel

title: Add 'When to Use' comparison guide for each algorithm category
description: Students don't know WHEN to use which algorithm. Add comparison tables: 'Use Merge Sort when stability matters, Quick Sort when average performance matters, Heap Sort when worst-case guarantee needed.' For each category (sorting, graph shortest path, tree types, DP patterns), add a decision guide. Source: common interview framework.
acceptanceCriteria: Each category has a 'When to Use Which?' comparison section accessible from sidebar
Sorting comparison: stability, in-place, best/worst case, input characteristics
Graph shortest path: Dijkstra vs Bellman-Ford vs Floyd-Warshall decision tree
DP: when to use 1D vs 2D table, memoization vs tabulation decision

title: Add LSH and Cosine Similarity for AI/ML vector search category
description: EMERGING: LSH (Locality-Sensitive Hashing) is a core ANN algorithm alongside HNSW. Cosine Similarity is the default distance metric for embedding comparisons. Add both to the new AI/ML category. Source: https://www.pinecone.io/learn/series/faiss/hnsw/
acceptanceCriteria: LSH appears in AI/ML or Vector Search category
LSH visualization shows hash buckets grouping similar vectors
Cosine Similarity shows vector angle comparison in 2D
Description explains: 'Used for approximate nearest neighbor search in embedding spaces'

title: Add Interval Merge and Bit Manipulation pattern categories
description: IMPORTANT MISSING from NeetCode 150 and Blind 75. Interval Merge: merge overlapping intervals, insert interval, meeting rooms. Bit Manipulation: single number, counting bits, reverse bits. Both are full NeetCode categories with 5+ problems each.
acceptanceCriteria: Interval patterns category with at least 2 problems
Bit Manipulation category with at least 2 problems
Interval visualization shows timeline with merge animation
Bit visualization shows binary representation with bit operations

title: Add Topological Sort (Kahn's BFS variant) alongside existing DFS variant
description: We have DFS-based topological sort but NOT Kahn's BFS-based variant. MIT teaches both. Interview candidates should know both approaches. Add Kahn's algorithm showing in-degree counting + BFS queue processing.
acceptanceCriteria: Kahn's Algorithm appears as a separate option or variant selector under Topological Sort
Visualization shows in-degree counters per node
BFS queue processing animated
Comparison note: 'DFS variant detects cycles via back edges; Kahn's detects via remaining nodes'

title: Implement 6 learning path presets for curriculum navigation
description: Phase 6 curriculum design identified 6 learning paths. Implement as selectable presets that filter/order the algorithm dropdown: (1) University CS (12 weeks), (2) FAANG Interview (4 weeks), (3) Senior Engineer (2 weeks), (4) Competitive Programming (8 weeks), (5) Weekend Crash Course (2 days), (6) AI/ML Engineer (new for 2026). Each path shows progress and recommended order.
acceptanceCriteria: Learning Path selector visible at top of sidebar
Selecting a path filters algorithms to only those in the path
Algorithms shown in recommended order within the path
Progress bar shows completion within selected path
All 6 paths defined with specific algorithm lists

title: Add Skip List and Count-Min Sketch to probabilistic data structures category
description: EMERGING: Skip List (used in Redis sorted sets, alternative to balanced trees) and Count-Min Sketch (streaming frequency estimation). Both are production-critical. Source: https://redis.io/blog/count-min-sketch-the-art-and-science-of-estimating-stuff/
acceptanceCriteria: Skip List visualization shows multi-level linked list structure
Insert/search operations show traversal across levels
Count-Min Sketch shows 2D grid with hash function mappings
Real-world application notes for both (Redis, streaming analytics)

title: Add Segment Tree and Fenwick Tree (BIT) for competitive programming path
description: ADVANCED: Both are in VisuAlgo, ICPC syllabus, and competitive programming training. Segment Tree: range queries (sum, min, max) with lazy propagation. Fenwick Tree: prefix sums with O(log n) update. Source: https://visualgo.net/en (Segment Tree and Fenwick Tree modules)
acceptanceCriteria: Segment Tree visualization shows tree structure built from array
Range query highlighting shows which nodes are accessed
Fenwick Tree shows binary indexed tree structure
Update propagation animated for both
Comparison note: 'Fenwick is simpler but only supports prefix queries; Segment Tree is more general'
